title: Ear pressure or blockage
summary: Colds can swell the Eustachian tube and trap pressure or fluid behind the eardrum. Most cases resolve as nasal inflammation settles; pain, discharge or significant hearing loss may indicate an ear infection or another problem.
evidenceSnapshot: Time, swallowing and nasal symptom control are usually sufficient. Routine decongestants and antihistamines have not shown reliable benefit for middle-ear fluid in children.

interventions.name: Swallowing, yawning and chewing
interventions.finding: Can gently open the Eustachian tube and equalise pressure.
interventions.practical: Sip water, chew sugar-free gum if age-appropriate, or yawn.
interventions.mechanism: Activates muscles that open the tube.
interventions.cautions: Do not force pressure if painful.

interventions.name: Gentle autoinflation
interventions.finding: May help some older children and adults who can perform it safely.
interventions.practical: Pinch the nose and blow very gently against closed nostrils, or use a purpose-built balloon device.
interventions.mechanism: Raises nasopharyngeal pressure to open the tube.
interventions.cautions: Stop if painful; do not use with severe congestion, recent ear surgery or uncertainty.

interventions.name: Pain relief
interventions.finding: Paracetamol or ibuprofen can relieve earache while the cause settles.
interventions.cautions: Ear pain in young infants warrants assessment.

interventions.name: Decongestants or antihistamines
interventions.finding: May help nasal symptoms in selected adults but do not reliably clear middle-ear fluid, especially in children.
interventions.practical: Do not give children cold medicines for this purpose.
interventions.cautions: Usual cardiovascular, sedation and rebound cautions.

practicalSteps: Avoid inserting cotton buds or drops unless the eardrum and diagnosis are known.
Keep the child upright for comfort and monitor hearing and balance.
Reassess if pain or fever is increasing rather than settling.
avoidOrCare: Ear discharge can mean a perforated eardrum or outer-ear infection.
Sudden hearing loss is urgent even without pain.

myths.claim: The ear needs to be cleaned out with a cotton bud.
myths.reality: Cold-related pressure is behind the eardrum; inserting objects can injure the canal and push wax deeper.

myths.claim: Antibiotics are always needed for ear pain.
myths.reality: Many middle-ear infections and pressure problems resolve without antibiotics; assessment depends on age, severity and findings.

redFlags: Swelling/redness behind the ear or ear pushed outward
Severe dizziness, facial weakness or neurological symptoms
Discharge with significant pain or fever
Young infant with fever or marked distress
unsettled: Evidence for home autoinflation during acute viral illness is limited and technique-dependent.
